title: Street Art: Vandalism or Culture?
summary: A narrator examines the ongoing debate about whether street art is a form of vandalism or a legitimate cultural expression.
body: Walk through almost any major city in the world and you will encounter street art. It appears on railway bridges, the sides of abandoned buildings, underpasses, and even the shutters of closed shops. For some people, it is an eyesore — unwanted graffiti that defaces public and private property without permission. For others, it is a vibrant and democratic art form that belongs to the streets and to everyone. The debate about street art is not new, but it has intensified in recent decades as the genre has moved from the margins into mainstream culture. Artists like Banksy, whose stencilled works comment on politics, consumerism, and social inequality, have attracted global audiences and critical attention. Some of his pieces have sold at auction for hundreds of thousands of pounds. Yet Banksy himself has always refused to operate within the formal art world, insisting that art should be accessible to all, not locked behind gallery walls. Supporters of street art argue that it transforms dull urban environments into open-air galleries. In cities like Melbourne, Bristol, and São Paulo, entire neighbourhoods have been reinvented through large-scale murals that attract visitors from around the world. Local authorities in these places have not only tolerated street art but actively commissioned it, recognising its economic and cultural value. Critics, however, point out that not all street art is equal. A thoughtful mural painted on a wall with the property owner's consent is quite different from a tag sprayed on a war memorial or a historical building. The line between art and vandalism, they argue, is not about style or creativity — it is about permission and context. There is also the question of who decides what counts as art. When a wealthy collector pays a large sum for a Banksy print, does that validate the work, or does it undermine the anti-establishment message the artist intended? Some street artists feel that commercial success and institutional recognition represent a kind of defeat — a betrayal of the original spirit of the movement. Despite these tensions, street art continues to flourish. New generations of artists are finding ways to engage with public spaces that go beyond spray paint, using installations, mosaic tiles, and even knitting. Whether you admire it or resent it, street art forces a conversation about who our cities belong to and whose voices are allowed to shape them.

annotations.exampleEn: You will encounter many different opinions when you discuss this topic.

annotations.meaningVi: làm hỏng bề mặt, phá hoại ngoại quan
annotations.exampleEn: Spraying graffiti on public property defaces it and is illegal.

annotations.meaningVi: sống động, tràn đầy sức sống
annotations.exampleEn: The city has a vibrant street art scene that draws tourists.

annotations.meaningVi: trở nên gay gắt hơn, mạnh mẽ hơn
annotations.exampleEn: The debate intensified after the new law was announced.

annotations.meaningVi: văn hóa chính thống, phổ thông
annotations.exampleEn: Hip-hop music moved from the margins into mainstream culture over several decades.

annotations.meaningVi: chủ nghĩa tiêu dùng, văn hóa mua sắm thái quá
annotations.exampleEn: Many artists use their work to criticise consumerism and materialism.
The documentary explored the links between poverty and social inequality.

annotations.meaningVi: dễ tiếp cận với mọi người
annotations.exampleEn: Public libraries make literature accessible to all citizens.

annotations.meaningVi: được đổi mới hoàn toàn, được tái tạo
annotations.exampleEn: The old warehouse district was reinvented as a creative hub.

annotations.meaningVi: đặt hàng, thuê người thực hiện
annotations.exampleEn: The city council commissioned a local artist to paint a mural on the library wall.

annotations.meaningVi: sự đồng ý, sự cho phép
annotations.exampleEn: You cannot use someone's image without their consent.

annotations.meaningVi: xác nhận giá trị, chứng minh tính hợp lệ
annotations.exampleEn: Does winning a prize validate an artist's work?

annotations.meaningVi: phản cơ chế, chống lại trật tự quyền lực hiện có
annotations.exampleEn: The band became famous for their anti-establishment lyrics.

annotations.meaningVi: phát triển mạnh mẽ, thịnh vượng
annotations.exampleEn: Small businesses can flourish when local communities support them.

annotations.phrase: whose voices are allowed to shape them
annotations.meaningVi: tiếng nói của ai được phép định hình chúng
annotations.pronunciation: /huːz ˈvɔɪsɪz ə əˈlaʊd tə ʃeɪp ðəm/
annotations.exampleEn: Urban planning should consider whose voices are allowed to shape the city.

grammarNotes.title: Contrast Connectors — however, yet, while, but
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài sử dụng nhiều từ nối tương phản để trình bày hai quan điểm đối lập: 'For some people ... For others', 'Supporters ... Critics, however', 'Yet Banksy himself', 'Some street artists feel ... Despite these tensions'. Ở cấp B1, học viên cần nắm vững các từ nối tương phản như 'however', 'yet', 'while', 'despite', 'although' để viết và nói một cách lưu loát về các quan điểm trái chiều. Vị trí của 'however' (thường đứng sau dấu chấm phẩy hoặc đầu câu) khác với 'but' (giữa câu).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses contrast connectors to present opposing viewpoints: 'For some people ... For others', 'Supporters ... Critics, however', 'Yet Banksy himself', 'Despite these tensions'. At B1 level, learners should master contrast connectors such as 'however', 'yet', 'while', 'despite', and 'although' to discuss opposing views fluently. Note that 'however' typically follows a semicolon or starts a new sentence, while 'but' sits mid-sentence.

grammarNotes.title: Passive Voice for Describing Changes and Actions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng bị động nhiều lần khi mô tả sự thay đổi và quyết định mà không cần chỉ định rõ chủ thể: 'entire neighbourhoods have been reinvented', 'street art has been... actively commissioned', 'a mural painted on a wall with the property owner's consent'. Cấu trúc 'have/has been + past participle' là bị động hoàn thành, thể hiện sự thay đổi đã xảy ra và còn ảnh hưởng đến hiện tại. Đây là cấu trúc phổ biến trong văn bản thảo luận và mô tả.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses passive voice when describing changes and decisions without specifying the agent: 'entire neighbourhoods have been reinvented', 'street art has been actively commissioned', 'a mural painted on a wall'. The 'have/has been + past participle' structure is the present perfect passive — it shows a change that happened and still affects the present. This is common in discussion and descriptive writing.

translationVi:
Đi bộ qua hầu hết các thành phố lớn trên thế giới, bạn sẽ bắt gặp nghệ thuật đường phố. Nó xuất hiện trên các cầu đường sắt, tường của những tòa nhà bỏ hoang, các đường hầm dành cho người đi bộ, và ngay cả cửa cuốn của những cửa hàng đóng cửa. Với một số người, đó là thứ gây khó chịu về thị giác — tranh vẽ graffiti không mong muốn làm hỏng tài sản công cộng và tư nhân mà không có sự cho phép. Với người khác, đó là một hình thức nghệ thuật sống động và dân chủ thuộc về đường phố và thuộc về tất cả mọi người.

Cuộc tranh luận về nghệ thuật đường phố không phải là mới, nhưng nó đã trở nên gay gắt hơn trong những thập kỷ gần đây khi thể loại này đã chuyển từ ngoài lề vào văn hóa chính thống. Các nghệ sĩ như Banksy, người có những tác phẩm stencil bình luận về chính trị, chủ nghĩa tiêu dùng và bất bình đẳng xã hội, đã thu hút khán giả toàn cầu và sự chú ý của giới phê bình. Một số tác phẩm của anh ấy đã được bán đấu giá với giá hàng trăm nghìn bảng Anh. Tuy nhiên, bản thân Banksy luôn từ chối hoạt động trong thế giới nghệ thuật chính thống, nhấn mạnh rằng nghệ thuật nên dễ tiếp cận với tất cả mọi người, không bị khóa sau những bức tường phòng tranh.

Những người ủng hộ nghệ thuật đường phố lập luận rằng nó biến đổi những môi trường đô thị buồn tẻ thành những phòng trưng bày ngoài trời. Ở những thành phố như Melbourne, Bristol và São Paulo, toàn bộ các khu phố đã được đổi mới hoàn toàn thông qua các bức tranh tường quy mô lớn thu hút du khách từ khắp nơi trên thế giới. Chính quyền địa phương ở những nơi này không chỉ chấp nhận nghệ thuật đường phố mà còn chủ động đặt hàng, công nhận giá trị kinh tế và văn hóa của nó.

Tuy nhiên, những người phê bình chỉ ra rằng không phải tất cả nghệ thuật đường phố đều như nhau. Một bức tranh tường được vẽ có suy nghĩ trên bức tường với sự đồng ý của chủ sở hữu tài sản là hoàn toàn khác với một thẻ tag được xịt lên một đài tưởng niệm chiến tranh hay một tòa nhà lịch sử. Ranh giới giữa nghệ thuật và phá hoại, họ lập luận, không phải về phong cách hay sự sáng tạo — mà là về sự cho phép và bối cảnh.

Cũng có câu hỏi về việc ai quyết định điều gì được coi là nghệ thuật. Khi một nhà sưu tập giàu có trả một khoản tiền lớn cho một bản in Banksy, điều đó có xác nhận giá trị của tác phẩm không, hay nó làm suy yếu thông điệp chống cơ chế mà nghệ sĩ muốn truyền đạt? Một số nghệ sĩ đường phố cảm thấy rằng thành công thương mại và sự công nhận từ tổ chức đại diện cho một loại thất bại — sự phản bội tinh thần ban đầu của phong trào.

Bất chấp những căng thẳng này, nghệ thuật đường phố tiếp tục phát triển mạnh mẽ. Các thế hệ nghệ sĩ mới đang tìm cách tương tác với không gian công cộng vượt ra ngoài sơn xịt, sử dụng các tác phẩm cài đặt, gạch mosaic, và thậm chí dệt len. Dù bạn ngưỡng mộ hay phẫn nộ về nó, nghệ thuật đường phố buộc một cuộc trò chuyện về việc các thành phố của chúng ta thuộc về ai và tiếng nói của ai được phép định hình chúng.

questions.prompt: Where does street art typically appear in cities?
options: On railway bridges, abandoned buildings, underpasses, and shop shutters
In parks and open green spaces
questions.explanation: The narrator says street art appears on 'railway bridges, the sides of abandoned buildings, underpasses, and even the shutters of closed shops.'
questions.hint: The narrator gives a list of specific places in the opening paragraph.

questions.prompt: How does the narrator describe street art for people who oppose it?
options: An interesting but misunderstood art form
An eyesore that defaces property without permission
questions.explanation: The narrator says for some people it is 'an eyesore — unwanted graffiti that defaces public and private property without permission.'
questions.hint: The narrator gives both sides of the argument in the first paragraph.

questions.prompt: What subjects do Banksy's works typically comment on?
questions.explanation: The narrator says Banksy's 'stencilled works comment on politics, consumerism, and social inequality.'
questions.hint: The narrator describes the themes in Banksy's work.

questions.prompt: Why does Banksy refuse to operate within the formal art world?
options: Because gallery owners refuse to exhibit his work
Because he believes art should be accessible to all, not locked behind gallery walls
Because he earns more money selling work on the street
Because he prefers to remain anonymous
questions.explanation: The narrator says Banksy insists 'that art should be accessible to all, not locked behind gallery walls.'
questions.hint: The narrator quotes Banksy's core belief about art.

questions.prompt: Which cities are mentioned as examples where neighbourhoods have been transformed by street art?
questions.explanation: The narrator mentions 'cities like Melbourne, Bristol, and São Paulo' where 'entire neighbourhoods have been reinvented through large-scale murals.'
questions.hint: The narrator gives three specific cities as examples.

questions.prompt: What have local authorities in these cities done regarding street art?
options: Banned all street art and introduced heavy fines
Ignored street art and left it to property owners
Tolerated and even actively commissioned street art
Created special museums to house street art indoors
questions.explanation: The narrator says authorities 'have not only tolerated street art but actively commissioned it, recognising its economic and cultural value.'
questions.hint: The narrator describes a progressive stance from these local authorities.

questions.prompt: According to the critics, what is the real difference between art and vandalism?
options: The quality and skill of the artwork
Whether the work uses colour or is black and white
questions.explanation: The narrator says critics argue that the line between art and vandalism 'is not about style or creativity — it is about permission and context.'
questions.hint: The narrator quotes critics' view directly.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator suggest might happen when a wealthy collector buys a Banksy print at auction?
options: It proves that street art is as good as traditional art
It may undermine the anti-establishment message the artist intended
It allows Banksy to fund more street art projects
It encourages other street artists to seek gallery representation
questions.explanation: The narrator asks whether commercial sale 'undermines the anti-establishment message the artist intended.'
questions.hint: The narrator raises a question about how commercial success affects artistic intent.

questions.prompt: How do some street artists view commercial success and institutional recognition?
options: As the ultimate goal they have been working towards
As a betrayal of the original spirit of the movement
As a sign that street art has matured as an art form
questions.explanation: The narrator says some street artists see commercial success as 'a betrayal of the original spirit of the movement.'
questions.hint: The narrator describes the reaction of artists who reject mainstream recognition.

questions.prompt: What new materials and methods are new generations of street artists using?
options: Oil paint on canvas placed in public spaces
questions.explanation: The narrator says new artists are 'using installations, mosaic tiles, and even knitting.'
questions.hint: The narrator gives specific examples of new techniques beyond spray paint.

questions.prompt: What broader question does the narrator say street art forces us to consider?
options: Who should decide what is considered beautiful
How much public money should be spent on the arts
Who our cities belong to and whose voices shape them
Whether art education should be compulsory in schools
questions.explanation: The narrator concludes that street art 'forces a conversation about who our cities belong to and whose voices are allowed to shape them.'
questions.hint: This is the final and most important point the narrator makes.

questions.prompt: How long has the debate about street art been going on?
options: It began only in the last few years
It is a new debate that emerged with social media
It is not a new debate, though it has intensified in recent decades
It dates back to the ancient world
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'The debate about street art is not new, but it has intensified in recent decades.'
questions.hint: The narrator makes this point clearly in the second paragraph.

cloze.template: Walk through almost any major city in the world and you will {{b1}} street art. It appears on railway bridges, the sides of abandoned buildings, underpasses, and even the shutters of closed shops. For some people, it is an {{b2}} — unwanted graffiti that defaces public and private property without permission. For others, it is a vibrant and democratic art form that belongs to the streets and to everyone. The debate about street art is not new, but it has {{b3}} in recent decades as the genre has moved from the margins into mainstream culture. Artists like Banksy, whose stencilled works comment on politics, {{b4}}, and social inequality, have attracted global audiences and critical attention. Yet Banksy himself has always refused to operate within the formal art world, insisting that art should be {{b5}} to all, not locked behind gallery walls. Supporters of street art argue that it transforms dull urban environments into open-air galleries. In cities like Melbourne, Bristol, and São Paulo, entire neighbourhoods have been {{b6}} through large-scale murals that attract visitors from around the world. Local authorities in these places have not only tolerated street art but actively {{b7}} it, recognising its economic and cultural value. Critics, however, point out that not all street art is equal. A thoughtful mural painted on a wall with the property owner's {{b8}} is quite different from a tag sprayed on a war memorial or a historical building. The line between art and vandalism, they argue, is about permission and context. Some street artists feel that commercial success and institutional recognition represent a kind of defeat — a betrayal of the original {{b9}} of the movement. Despite these tensions, street art continues to {{b10}}. New generations of artists are finding ways to engage with public spaces that go beyond spray paint, using installations, mosaic tiles, and even knitting.

blanks.explanation: 'Encounter' means to come across or meet unexpectedly — 'you will encounter street art' is the exact phrase used.
'Eyesore' is the specific word used — it means something ugly that is unpleasant to look at.
'Intensified' means become stronger or more extreme — 'the debate has intensified in recent decades'.
'Consumerism' is the specific term used — it refers to the cultural focus on buying and consuming goods.
'Accessible to all' is the phrase — meaning available and reachable for everyone, not just the privileged.
'Reinvented' means completely reimagined and given a new identity — stronger than simply 'changed' or 'improved'.
'Commissioned' means officially requested and paid for — local authorities 'actively commissioned' the art.
'Consent' is the legal and formal word for agreement — 'with the property owner's consent' is the phrase used.
'Spirit' in this context means the essential character or ethos — 'the original spirit of the movement'.
'Flourish' means to develop and thrive in a healthy, vigorous way — 'street art continues to flourish'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator asks who gets to decide what counts as art. Do you think the location and context of an artwork — whether it is in a gallery, on a street corner, or on a war memorial — changes its value or meaning? Who should have the power to make that judgment?
